preFix.title: Smoked salmon sandwich
preFix.subtitle: Cold-smoked salmon on brown bread with cream cheese and lemon
preFix.excerpt: A smoked salmon sandwich on brown bread with cream cheese, lemon, and black pepper is one of the best things you can make in five minutes. The combination of cold, silky salmon and the slight sharpness of cream cheese on grainy bread is close to perfect.
preFix.sourceNotes: British smoked salmon is a traditional product with Scottish and Irish origins, cold-smoked over oak. The smoked salmon sandwich became a fixture of British catering and celebration food from the mid-20th century, associated with special occasions and upmarket cafes.

content.text: The smoked salmon sandwich is best made with brown or wholemeal bread: the nuttiness of the bread complements the salt of the salmon in a way that white bread does not. Cream cheese is the standard spread, but a very thin layer of good salted butter also works. Lemon juice squeezed over the salmon just before assembly brightens everything. Capers are traditional and optional.
Spread a generous layer of cream cheese on all four slices of bread.
Lay the smoked salmon over two slices, covering the cream cheese. Squeeze the lemon juice over the salmon. Season with black pepper. Add capers and dill fronds if using.
Top with the remaining bread, cream-cheese side down. Press gently and cut diagonally. Serve immediately or keep covered and chilled for up to 30 minutes.

items.fix: Use a plain full-fat cream cheese with no added salt, and be sparing with any additional seasoning.
items.cause: Smoked salmon is naturally high in salt; cream cheese added additional salt.

items.fix: Make close to serving time and use just a small squeeze of lemon.
items.cause: Assembled too far ahead, or too much lemon juice was squeezed on.

items.fix: Press the salmon lightly into the cream cheese so it adheres. Thin slices stay in place better.
items.cause: Slices were too thick or not pressed into the cream cheese.
items.symptom: The salmon slides out when you bite

content.text: Cold-smoked salmon has been produced in Scotland and Ireland for centuries, first as a method of preservation. The smoked salmon sandwich became part of British celebration food in the 20th century, at wedding receptions, Christmas parties, and upmarket cafes. It is now in every supermarket year-round. It has moved from special-occasion food to everyday lunch, but still carries a sense of something slightly more considered.
